BBC News — 22 March 2026 — By Sarah Rainsford
Authentic Text

Italy is voting on whether to change its constitution.

What does this mean for Meloni?

B2 · Upper Intermediate Italian L1 Grammar: Reporting Verbs 8 Vocabulary Items
BBC News  ·  Italy constitutional referendum, March 2026  ·  By Sarah Rainsford

📚 Article

By Sarah Rainsford  ·  BBC News  ·  22 March 2026
🔊 Listen

In her push for a “yes” vote in this weekend’s constitutional referendum, Italy’s prime minister has been reaching for new audiences. On Thursday, Giorgia Meloni sat at the mic between a popular rapper and a personal trainer-turned-podcaster to urge Italians to vote for a judicial system she promised would be “more just” for all.

Opposition parties have been busy too, styling the “historic” reform proposed by the right-wing government as a threat to democracy, and turning it into a protest vote against Meloni’s rule. In power for three-and-a-half years, close to a record in a land of shaky coalitions, Meloni prides herself on bringing stability to Italy. But as this referendum has become increasingly personalised, she could be facing her first significant defeat.

“She wants to win. If she loses, there will be an impact and she understands that,” political scientist Roberto D’Alimonte of Luiss University explains. “She needs to mobilise her voters to have a better chance to win this race. But it’s going to be touch and go.”

So on air Meloni set out her case for “yes” with trademark gusto. She said changes to how Italy’s judges and prosecutors work would make the system “more meritocratic, more responsible, more efficient”. She also argued her opponents were only turning all this into a referendum on her rule because they couldn’t fault the reform itself. In any case, Meloni made clear, she wouldn’t be stepping down. “If you vote ‘no’ today just to send Meloni home, you’ll find yourself keeping both Meloni and a justice system that doesn’t work,” the prime minister said. “That doesn’t seem like a good deal to me.”

So is Italian justice really “broken”? The system is certainly chronically overloaded, with some of the longest-running trials in the EU. The referendum looks at separating the career paths of judges and prosecutors, as well as creating distinct bodies to govern them.

The proposal is a project of the late Silvio Berlusconi’s party, Forza Italia, part of the current coalition. Meloni herself has also clashed publicly with the courts, claiming that “powerful factions” routinely derail her signature policies, especially on migration. Such rhetoric is one reason why opposition parties are so wary of this reform. They fear Meloni’s real aim is to subjugate the judiciary to her political control. She and her team deny that.

But at a time of global uncertainty and rising prices, they also know voters may use the referendum to vent their frustration. “The geopolitical situation is very tough, of course. People are scared. I completely feel that. But we need to stay focused on the purpose of the referendum,” Andrea di Giuseppe, a member of Meloni’s Fratelli d’Italia party, argues.

There are some, even on the left wing of politics, who support this reform and see the close ties between judges and prosecutors as too cosy. On the other hand, this would be the first time a hard right government has changed Italy’s constitution, written in the 1940s after the defeat of fascism.

If Meloni wins this vote she has grand political ambitions, including increasing the powers of her own office in a future referendum. But the much-vaunted stability she has brought to Italy would be shaken at a particularly vulnerable time if she loses. “She will not resign,” the professor says. “But she will be politically weakened, there is no question about that.”

📚 Vocabulary & Pronunciation

Click any word to hear it and see notes below.

Word ▶PronunciationPart of speechMeaningIn context
▶ subjugate SUB-juh-gayt verb To bring someone or something under complete control They fear Meloni's real aim is to subjugate the judiciary to her political control.
▶ rhetoric RET-uh-rik noun Persuasive language — often exaggerated or used to impress rather than inform Such rhetoric is one reason why opposition parties are so wary of this reform.
▶ wary WAIR-ee adjective Cautious and watchful, especially about possible danger or problems Opposition parties are so wary of this reform.
▶ vaunted VAWN-tid adjective Talked about with great pride — sometimes more than is deserved The much-vaunted stability she has brought to Italy would be shaken.
▶ meritocratic meh-rih-tuh-KRAT-ik adjective Based on ability and hard work rather than privilege or connections Changes would make the system "more meritocratic, more responsible."
▶ coalition koh-uh-LIH-shun noun A temporary alliance of parties or groups who govern together Close to a record in a land of shaky coalitions.
▶ chronically KRON-ik-lee adverb In a way that has continued for a long time and is hard to change The system is certainly chronically overloaded.
▶ vent vent verb To express strong feelings — especially frustration or anger Voters may use the referendum to vent their frustration.

Click a word above to hear it and see notes.

💬 Expressions & Idioms

These phrases from the article are worth learning as fixed units.

touch and go
Uncertain — the outcome could go either way
“It’s going to be touch and go.”
step down
To resign from a position of power
“She wouldn’t be stepping down.”
reach for new audiences
To try to attract or appeal to people you haven’t reached before
“Meloni has been reaching for new audiences.”
in any case
Whatever happens; regardless of the situation
“In any case, Meloni made clear she wouldn’t resign.”
vent frustration
To express anger or frustration openly
“Voters may use the referendum to vent their frustration.”
kick off the bus
Informal: to remove someone from power; vote them out
“In 2027 you can just kick it off the bus.”

💡 Grammar Spotlight

Words we use instead of ‘say’ — and why they matter

In journalism, writers use many different verbs to show what someone said. Each one sends a slightly different signal about how confident, aggressive, or neutral that person was being. At B2, it is worth building up a bank of these.

explains — the speaker is giving reasons or background calmly:

“D’Alimonte explains that she needs to mobilise her voters.” — presenting information clearly

argues — the speaker is making a case, pushing a point of view:

“Di Giuseppe argues that voters should focus on the referendum itself.”

claims — the speaker is asserting something, but the writer is slightly sceptical:

“Meloni claimed — with no evidence — that rejecting the reform would mean more criminals being freed.”

denies — the speaker is rejecting an accusation directly:

“She and her team deny that her aim is to control the judiciary.”

A note for Italian speakers: The word claims is useful to recognise, but be careful using it yourself. In English it carries a hint of doubt — it is not neutral like dice or afferma. Using claims instead of says in your own writing can make you sound sceptical of your source, which may not be what you intend.

📚 Try this: find three more ‘says’ alternatives in the article

🇮🇹 Language Notes for Italian Speakers

Watch outrhetoric / retorica — In Italian, retorica can be neutral or even positive (the art of persuasion, a school subject). In English, rhetoric nearly always has a negative meaning — language that sounds impressive but may be empty or misleading. Do not use them as direct equivalents.
Watch outwary / aware — These two words look similar but mean very different things. Wary = cautious, on your guard (diffidente / prudente). Aware = conscious of something (consapevole). The preposition is always wary of, never *wary about.
Watch outclaims as a reporting verb — In English, using claims suggests the writer is not sure whether something is true. It is not neutral. In your own writing, use says or states unless you genuinely want to suggest doubt.
Collocationshaky coalition — English describes an unstable political alliance as shaky or fragile, not *weak (influenced by coalizione debole). Other useful collocations: form a coalition, coalition government, coalition partner.
Good newsmeritocratic / meritocrazia — A safe and direct cognate. Stress falls in the same position in both languages: me-ri-to-KRAT-ic. Use this as a model for the -cracy / -crazia suffix: democracy, bureaucracy, autocracy.
Good newscoalition / coalizione — Direct cognate, safe transfer. The main difference is stress: English stresses the third syllable (koh-uh-LIH-shun); Italian the fourth (co-a-li-ZIO-ne).
